sections.title: Lesson 1: Rashid & Rana Designs
sections.explanation: Email about starting a new clothing company.
sections.notes: Focus on past simple tense for completed actions in the past.

examples.problem: Rana _______ an email to Bidor to tell her about her new clothes company.
She and her brother, Rashid, _______ the company last September.
They _______ to Lebanon and Morocco to choose cloth two months ago.
They _______ a lot of success at London Fashion Week.

sections.title: Grammar: Past Simple Questions
sections.definition: Forming questions in past simple tense.
sections.explanation: Use 'did' + subject + base form of verb for questions about past events.
sections.notes: Questions about trips: preferred country, travel companions, food, hotels, sightseeing, language used.

examples.problem: Ask about the country she preferred.
examples.solution: Which country did you prefer, Lebanon or Morocco?

examples.problem: Ask about who travelled with her.
Ask about the food.

sections.title: Grammar: Past Continuous vs Past Simple
sections.definition: Past continuous: action in progress. Past simple: completed action.
sections.explanation: Use past continuous for the longer action, past simple for the interrupting action.

examples.problem: I (sit) in the park when somebody (kick) a ball at me.
We (drive) to the beach when we (hear) a loud crash.
Khaled (play) football when he (break) his ankle.

sections.title: Writing: Email About a New Company
sections.definition: Write an email telling a friend about a new business.
sections.explanation: Model your email on Rana's email. Include details about the company, products, and recent activities.
sections.notes: Suggested products: jewellery, cosmetics, perfume, food, footwear.

sections.title: Lesson 2: People didn't use to work out at home
sections.definition: Article about how fitness has changed with technology.
sections.explanation: Technology has made home workouts easier with apps, smart equipment, and virtual classes.
sections.notes: Gyms now compete with home fitness options.

examples.problem: Why is it now easier for people to work out from home?
examples.solution: Because of fitness apps, smart equipment, and online classes.

examples.problem: What are some ways technology has changed fitness?
examples.solution: Apps track workouts, mirrors show exercises, AI provides data.

sections.title: Grammar: Used to / Didn't use to
sections.definition: Used to expresses past habits or states that are no longer true.
sections.explanation: Use 'used to' for things that were true in the past but are not true now. Use 'didn't use to' for negative.
sections.notes: Compare how a gym looked before and now.

examples.problem: What equipment did you use to have in the gym?
examples.solution: We used to have old treadmills, but now we have smart machines.

sections.title: Grammar: Have to, Don't have to, Mustn't, Should, Shouldn't
sections.definition: Expressing obligation, no obligation, prohibition, and advice.
sections.explanation: Have to: external obligation. Don't have to: no obligation. Mustn't: prohibition. Should: advice. Shouldn't: negative advice.
sections.notes: Birchwood International College rules for new students.

examples.problem: You _______ share a room with other students, but the price is lower if you do.
All students _______ be out of their rooms by 9:30 a.m.
Students _______ be late.
You _______ take the special computer course, but we think it's useful.

sections.title: Grammar: Past Regrets - I wish, If only, Third Conditional
sections.explanation: I wish + past perfect: regret about past. If only + past perfect: strong regret. Third conditional: if + past perfect, would have + past participle.
rules: I wish + had + past participle - expresses regret about something in the past.
If only + had + past participle - expresses stronger regret.
If + past perfect, would have + past participle - expresses hypothetical past result.
sections.notes: Used when we want to change something that already happened.

examples.problem: I bet you wish you (not eat) so much now.
If only I (not forget) my key when I left home.
Now I wish I (buy) them earlier, when they were cheaper.
If you had listened to me, you (bring) the map.

sections.title: Writing: Past Regrets
sections.definition: Complete sentences to express regret using I wish, If only, or third conditional.
sections.explanation: Use past perfect after I wish/If only. Use third conditional structure for hypothetical past situations.
sections.notes: Express what you would have done differently.

examples.problem: I didn't buy the jacket while it was on sale.
examples.solution: If only I had bought the jacket while it was on sale.

examples.problem: My dad didn't check the oil, and the car broke down.
examples.solution: The car wouldn't have broken down if he had checked the oil.

examples.problem: Qais didn't make a copy of his essay, so he had to write it all again.
examples.solution: If Qais had made a copy of his essay, he wouldn't have had to write it all again.

sections.title: Speaking: What would you have done?
sections.explanation: Consider different scenarios and say what you would have done differently.
sections.notes: Use third conditional and 'would have' to express alternative actions.

examples.problem: Ahmed was answering a phone call on a bus. People next to him started singing loudly. He asked them to stop.
examples.solution: I would have moved to a different seat. If the bus had been full, I would have complained to the driver.

sections.title: Unit 4 Test - Vocabulary
sections.definition: Match vocabulary words with their definitions.
sections.explanation: Words related to fitness, business, and personal qualities.
sections.notes: Vocabulary includes: personal trainer, profit, previous experience, fitness app, punctual, designer, ambitious, stationary bike, score, personal assistant, calories, compete.

examples.problem: a professional who supervises how you exercise in a gym
the money you make in a business
always arriving on time

sections.title: Unit 4 Test - Conditionals
sections.definition: Complete sentences with correct conditional forms.
sections.explanation: Use second conditional for hypothetical present/future, first conditional for real possibilities, third conditional for past hypotheticals.
sections.notes: Second conditional: if + past, would + infinitive. Third conditional: if + past perfect, would have + past participle.

examples.problem: If I (be) you, I would apply for that course this week.
I (help) you with your application form if you bring it round on Saturday.
Ali would be really happy if he (can) spend the summer studying in London.

sections.title: Unit 4 Test - Word Formation
sections.notes: Suffixes change word forms (nouns, adjectives, verbs).

examples.problem: fract
ineffi
identi
applica

sections.title: Unit 4 Test - Gap Fill
sections.definition: Complete sentences with one word in each gap.
sections.explanation: Focus on grammar structures: modals, conditionals, and causative have.
sections.notes: Includes have to, would have, if clauses, should, shall, could, and causative have/get.

examples.problem: You don't have _______ go to the office every day in this job.
You would _______ passed the driving test if you hadn't made that mistake.
If you _______ not feeling better by tomorrow, you can stay at home.
We _______ our house painted this week.

sections.title: Unit 4 Test - Error Correction
sections.definition: Find and correct mistakes in sentences.
sections.explanation: Common errors: tense consistency, used to form, causative structure, conditional structure.
sections.notes: Review past continuous vs past simple, used to, causative have/get, and third conditional.

examples.problem: We played football when it started to rain, and we had to stop.
examples.solution: We were playing football when it started to rain, and we had to stop.

examples.problem: This gym used to had the best equipment in town, but not anymore.
examples.solution: This gym used to have the best equipment in town, but not anymore.

examples.problem: Amjed didn't used to work out, but now he's really fit.
examples.solution: Amjed didn't use to work out, but now he's really fit.

examples.problem: If only you told me about your problem before. I could have helped you.
examples.solution: If only you had told me about your problem before. I could have helped you.
